我的人工智能辅助工作流程

My AI-Assisted Workflow

www.maiobarbero.dev 作者 maiobarbero 分数 85 评论 111 2026/04/15 15:08
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 作为技术主管，我如何构建结构化的人工智能辅助开发工作流程，其中真正的工作发生在编写一行代码之前。您打开一个聊天，描述您想要什么，迭代输出，然后交付或多或少有效的东西。感觉很快。从技术上讲，这些功能是有效的。但没有人，包括我在内，完全理解其中的内容。没有人想到处理边缘情况，架构在当时有意义，但无法在与下一个功能的接触中幸存下来。我越来越感觉自己构建得越来越快，理解却越来越少。我一直遇到的问题是：如何获得人工智能辅助的速度优势，同时又不失去使软件可维护的清晰度和意向性？简而言之，真正的工作发生在编码开始之前。核心思想：用文字思考，而不是用代码思考 AI 到底擅长什么？执行。它真正不擅长什么？弄清楚你真正想要什么，找出你忘记明确表达的假设，并在你对问题的思维模型错误时告诉你。那是你的工作。这将永远是你的工作。我所做的最有价值的转变是将每个功能首先视为思考问题，然后是实施问题。该工作流程旨在强制在编写任何代码之前进行这种思考，并使用人工智能对其进行压力测试，而不是跳过它。该工作流程已根据 Mark Pocock 的技能进行了调整，以便与我一起工作。工作流程 第 1 步：自由格式计划 一切都从我自己编写的文档开始，用简单的语言编写，没有必需的结构。我描述问题、我对解决方案的初步想法、我意识到的限制以及我不确定的事情。这不是可交付成果，除了我之外没有人会读。它的唯一目的是让我的头脑中的想法变成我可以检查的形式。下游一切的质量完全取决于这一步的质量。模糊的计划会产生模糊的 PRD，从而产生模糊的问题，产生的代码在技术上可以运行，但不会执行您的意思。步骤 2：通过 write-a-prd 进行 PRD 自由形式的计划成为结构化面试流程的输入。该技能探索代码库以了解事物的当前状态，然后不断地采访我有关计划的各个方面，沿着设计树的每个分支，逐一解决决策之间的依赖关系。这是坏想法被抓住的一步。并不是因为人工智能比我聪明，而是因为被迫回答有关你自己计划的具体问题，揭示了你挥手的地方。 “当用户未经身份验证时，这会如何表现？” “如果此操作部分失败会发生什么？” “你说这会取代现有的功能，那么依赖当前行为的用户会发生什么？”输出是一个结构化的 PRD 文件，其中包含问题陈述、解决方案描述、广泛的用户故事列表、实施决策（模块、接口、模式更改、API 合同）、模块设计、测试决策和明确的超出范围的项目。一切都是明确的。用户故事是接下来一切的支柱。它们需要足够具体，以便可以在下游从它们中明确地得出验收标准，不是在现阶段，而是在问题级别定义范围时。步骤 3：通过 PRD 到问题的问题 PRD 成为使用垂直切片、端到端地贯穿每个集成层而不是单个层的水平切片的示踪子弹的一组问题。仅涉及数据库或仅涉及 UI 的切片不是有效的切片。每个问题都应该提供一条狭窄但完整的路径，可以自行演示或验证。每个问题都被分类为 AFK（AI 可以实施并且更改可以在没有人类交互的情况下合并）或 HITL（在实施过程中的某个时刻需要人类决策）。尽可能选择 AFK 而不是 HITL，这样可以保持工作顺利进行，而不会成为我注意力的瓶颈。在编写任何内容之前，该技能会提出建议的细分并询问：粒度是否正确，依赖关系是否正确，是否应该合并或拆分任何内容？问题按依赖顺序编写，以便它们之间的交叉引用使用实数。每个问题都包含端到端行为的简明描述、准确描述如何确认切片完整的“如何验证”部分、Given/When/Then 格式的验收标准（包括错误案例）、阻止程序列表以及对其所处理的用户故事的引用。一切都存在于文件中。我在不同的平台上工作，有时是 GitHub，有时是 GitLab，保持工作流程基于文件意味着它不依赖于任何特定工具。第 4 步：通过问题到任务来确定任务 每个问题都被分解为具体的、有序的任务，每个重点 AI 会话对应一个任务。这个约束是有意为之的：如果一项任务不能在一次会话中完成，它太大了。该技能探索问题涉及的代码库的特定部分，识别要遵循的现有模式，并生成包含类型（写入、测试、迁移、配置、审核）、显式输出和依赖顺序的任务列表。架构在逻辑之前，逻辑在 API 之前，API 在 UI 之前，测试交错而不是最后批处理。任务描述中的关键设计决策：它们被编写为执行它们的人工智能的指令，而不是人类开发人员的注释。每个任务都指定要接触哪些文件、要遵循哪些现有模式以及完成后的输出。没有代码片段，只有意图，而不是实现。第 5 步：移交代码 每个任务描述都是一个独立的提示。当我准备好实施任务时，我会打开一个新会话，并将任务描述以及父问题粘贴到上下文中。任务描述就是为此目的而编写的，它指定了范围，引用了正确的文件和模式，并定义了完成的内容。每个任务都有新的上下文是有意为之的。积累上下文的长时间会话往往会产生偏差：模型开始根据它已经做了的事情而不是任务需要的内容来做出决策。从范围明确的任务开始，与持续进行长时间的任务相比，始终会产生更好的输出。对于审核任务，那些标记为需要人工决策的任务，我会停下来，做出决定，用结果更新任务文件，然后继续。这些是工作流程发挥作用的时刻：决策是在上下文中经过深思熟虑做出的，而不是埋藏在漫长的一代人中。第 6 步：通过代码审查进行代码审查 每个 PR 在合并之前都会经过结构化的六遍审查。这些通行证涵盖逻辑错误、操作顺序、不良实践、安全性、魔术字符串和值以及模式改进。在人工智能生成的代码中，操作顺序值得特别注意。模型往往会生成执行正确操作但有时顺序错误的代码：在提交事务之前发送通知，在应记录的操作之后写入审核日志，在验证输入之前改变状态。这些错误在审查时很容易被忽略，因为代码乍一看是正确的。审查针对文件或差异进行，而不是针对整个功能。范围被故意缩小，在公关层面发现问题比事后发现问题要便宜得多。第 7 步：通过最终审核进行最终审核 在功能结束时，横切审核会查看只能在整个实施过程中进行评估的内容。不是个别错误，这些错误应该按照 PR 捕获，而是系统性问题：模块之间的不一致、早期引入并在各处错误复制的模式、孤立存在但在整个表面区域中崩溃的安全假设。审计在标记任何内容之前会读取完整的实现，这就是重点。它按严重性对调查结果进行分组，对该功能是否可以安全地投入生产给出明确的总体判断，并在进行任何更改之前请求批准。对已合并代码进行无人监督的修复比开发期间进行的修复风险更大。这个工作流程不是什么 建立起来并不快。规划和 PRD 步骤需要实时进行，并且跳过这些步骤而直接编写代码的诱惑始终存在。只有当您真正相信编码前的思考时间比编码后的调试时间更便宜时，该工作流程才会有回报。它也不能替代工程判断。人工智能会在每一步提出合理的建议，但对于你的具体情况来说是错误的。在创建任何东西之前评估故障的审查步骤之所以存在，正是因为人工智能的输出需要根据它不具备的知识进行验证：您的团队的惯例、用户的实际行为、代码库中隐藏复杂性的部分。基本原理 此工作流程中的每个步骤都具有相同的结构：人工智能产生一些东西，您在完整的上下文中对其进行审查，然后它被创建。人工智能加速了生产。评论始终是您的。该工作流程旨在确保在评估问题时有一个 PRD 来检查它，从而使审查尽可能有效；当你评估一项任务时，你有一个需要检查的问题；当你审查代码时，你有验收标准来检查它。技能 现在，如果您读完本文，您至少应该获得我的技能的链接。检查我的 GitHub 存储库。阅读下一步

Original Summary

  1. How I built a structured AI-assisted development workflow as a Tech Lead, where the real work happens before a single line of code is written. You open a chat, describe what you want, iterate on the output, and ship something that more or less works. It feels fast. The features work, technically. But nobody, including me, fully understood what is there. Edge cases nobody thought to handle, architecture that made sense in the moment but didn’t survive contact with the next feature. A growing sense that I was building faster and understanding less. The problem I kept running into was this: how do you get the speed benefits of AI assistance without losing the clarity and intentionality that makes software maintainable? The short answer is that the real work happens before the coding starts. The core idea: thinking in writing, not in code What is AI actually good at? Implementation. What is it genuinely bad at? Figuring out what you actually want, catching the assumptions you forgot to make explicit, and telling you when your mental model of the problem is wrong. That’s your job. It will always be your job. The single most valuable shift I made was treating every feature as a thinking problem first and an implementation problem second. The workflow is designed to force that thinking to happen before any code is written, and to use AI to stress-test it, not to skip it. This workflow has been adapted to work with me from Mark Pocock’s skills. The workflow Step 1: Free-form plan Everything starts with a document I write myself, in plain language, with no required structure. I describe the problem, my initial thinking about the solution, the constraints I’m aware of, and the things I’m uncertain about. This is not a deliverable, nobody reads it but me. Its only purpose is to get the thinking out of my head and into a form I can examine. The quality of everything downstream depends entirely on the quality of this step. A vague plan produces a vague PRD, which produces vague issues, which produces code that technically runs but doesn’t do what you meant. Step 2: PRD via write-a-prd The free-form plan becomes the input to a structured interview process. The skill explores the codebase to understand the current state of things, then interviews me relentlessly about every aspect of the plan, walking down each branch of the design tree, resolving dependencies between decisions one by one. This is the step where bad ideas get caught. Not because the AI is smarter than me, but because being forced to answer specific questions about your own plan reveals the places where you were hand-waving. “How does this behave when the user isn’t authenticated?” “What happens if this operation partially fails?” “You said this replaces the existing feature, what happens to users who depend on the current behavior?” The output is a structured PRD file containing a problem statement, a solution description, an extensive list of user stories, implementation decisions (modules, interfaces, schema changes, API contracts), module design, testing decisions, and explicit out-of-scope items. Everything is explicit. The user stories are the backbone of everything that follows. They need to be specific enough that acceptance criteria can be derived from them unambiguously downstream, not at this stage, but when scope is defined at the issue level. Step 3: Issues via prd-to-issues The PRD becomes a set of issues using vertical slices, tracer bullets that cut through every integration layer end-to-end rather than horizontal slices of a single layer. A slice that only touches the database, or only touches the UI, is not a valid slice. Each issue should deliver a narrow but complete path that is demoable or verifiable on its own. Each issue is classified as either AFK (the AI can implement and the change can be merged without human interaction) or HITL (a human decision is required at some point during implementation). Preferring AFK over HITL wherever possible keeps the work moving without becoming a bottleneck on my attention. Before anything is written, the skill presents the proposed breakdown and asks: does the granularity feel right, are the dependency relationships correct, should anything be merged or split? Issues are written in dependency order so that cross-references between them use real numbers. Each issue contains a concise description of the end-to-end behavior, a “how to verify” section describing exactly how to confirm the slice is complete, acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then format including error cases, a list of blockers, and references back to the user stories it addresses. Everything lives in files. I work across different platforms, sometimes GitHub, sometimes GitLab, and keeping the workflow file-based means it doesn’t depend on any particular tool. Step 4: Tasks via issues-to-tasks Each issue is broken down into concrete, ordered tasks, one task per focused AI session. The constraint is deliberate: if a task can’t be completed in a single session, it’s too large. The skill explores the specific parts of the codebase touched by the issue, identifies existing patterns to follow, and produces a task list with types (WRITE, TEST, MIGRATE, CONFIG, REVIEW), explicit outputs, and dependency order. Schema before logic, logic before API, API before UI, tests interleaved rather than batched at the end. The key design decision in the task descriptions: they are written as instructions to the AI that will execute them, not as notes to a human developer. Each task specifies which files to touch, which existing patterns to follow, and what the output looks like when done. No code snippets, just intent, not implementation. Step 5: The handoff to code Each task description is a self-contained prompt. When I’m ready to implement a task, I open a fresh session and paste the task description along with the parent issue for context. The task description was written for this purpose, it specifies scope, references the right files and patterns, and defines what done looks like. Fresh context per task is intentional. Long sessions with accumulated context tend to drift: the model starts making decisions based on what it already did rather than what the task requires. Starting clean with a well-scoped task consistently produces better output than continuing a long session. For REVIEW tasks, the ones flagged as requiring a human decision, I stop, make the decision, update the task file with the outcome, and continue. These are the moments where the workflow earns its keep: the decision is made deliberately, in context, not buried in a long generation. Step 6: Code review via code-review Every PR goes through a structured six-pass review before merge. The passes cover logic errors, operation ordering, bad practices, security, magic strings and values, and pattern improvements. Operation ordering deserves particular attention in AI-generated code. Models tend to produce code that does the right things but sometimes in the wrong sequence: sending a notification before committing a transaction, writing an audit log after the action it should record, mutating state before validating input. These bugs are easy to miss in review because the code looks correct at a glance. The review runs on a file or diff, not on the whole feature. Scope is deliberately narrow, catching issues at the PR level is far cheaper than finding them later. Step 7: Final audit via final-audit At the end of a feature, a cross-cutting audit looks at things that can only be evaluated across the whole implementation. Not individual bugs, those should have been caught per PR, but systemic issues: inconsistencies between modules, patterns that were introduced early and replicated incorrectly everywhere, security assumptions that hold in isolation but break down across the full surface area. The audit reads the full implementation before flagging anything, which is the point. It groups findings by severity, gives an explicit overall verdict on whether the feature is safe to leave in production, and asks for approval before making any changes. Unsupervised fixes on already-merged code are riskier than fixes made during development. What this workflow is not It is not fast to set up. The planning and PRD steps take real time, and the temptation to skip them in favor of going straight to code is constant. The workflow only pays off if you genuinely believe that thinking time before coding is cheaper than debugging time after. It is also not a replacement for engineering judgment. The AI will suggest reasonable things at every step that are wrong for your specific situation. The review steps, where you evaluate the breakdown before anything is created, exist precisely because the AI’s output needs to be validated against knowledge it doesn’t have: your team’s conventions, your users’ actual behavior, the parts of the codebase that have hidden complexity. The underlying principle Every step in this workflow has the same structure: AI produces something, you review it with full context, then it gets created. The AI accelerates the production. The review is yours, always. The workflow is designed to make that review as effective as possible, by ensuring that when you’re evaluating an issue, you have a PRD to check it against; when you’re evaluating a task, you have an issue to check it against; and when you’re reviewing code, you have acceptance criteria to check it against. The skills Now if you read this article to the end you at least deserve a link with my skills. Check my GitHub repo. Read Next

评论（默认中文）

crustycoder 2026/04/15 18:19
他还错过了一个重要的步骤，即将你的技能输入LLM并要求它对其进行批评——毕竟，是LLM要对其采取行动，所以要求它首先进行评估是很重要的。我已经为他的技能做到了这一点，这是评估：

=========

  Bottom line
  Against the agentskills.io guidance, they look more like workflow specs than polished agent skills.
  The largest gap is not correctness. It is skill design discipline:

  # stronger descriptions,
  # lighter defaults,
  # less mandatory process,
  # better degraded-mode handling,
  # clearer evidence that the skills were refined through trigger/output evals.

  Skill           Score/10
  write-a-prd          5.4
  prd-to-issues        6.8
  issues-to-tasks      6.0
  code-review          7.6
  final-audit          6.3
=========

LLM元编程非常重要，我刚刚完成了LLM辅助设计文档创作会议，其中LLM的建议是“不要在该部分使用LLM，它不够可靠”。

He's also missed a major step, which is to feed your skill into the LLM and ask it to critique it - after all, it's the LLM that's going to act on it, so asking it to assess first is kinda important. I've done that for his skills, here's the assessment:

==========

  Bottom line
  Against the agentskills.io guidance, they look more like workflow specs than polished agent skills.
  The largest gap is not correctness. It is skill design discipline:

  # stronger descriptions,
  # lighter defaults,
  # less mandatory process,
  # better degraded-mode handling,
  # clearer evidence that the skills were refined through trigger/output evals.

  Skill           Score/10
  write-a-prd          5.4
  prd-to-issues        6.8
  issues-to-tasks      6.0
  code-review          7.6
  final-audit          6.3
==========

LLM metaprogramming is extremely important, I've just finished a LLM-assisted design doc authoring session where the recommendations of the LLM are "Don't use a LLM for that part, it won't be reliable enough".

fergonco 2026/04/15 18:58
>“不要在该部分使用法学硕士，它不够可靠”。

你现在应该问一下 LLM 是否足够可靠。

抛开笑话不谈，他怎么就错过了这一步呢？他正在利用这些技能来提高效率。违反 Agentskills.io 指导有多重要？

> "Don't use a LLM for that part, it won't be reliable enough".

You should now ask if the LLM is reliable enough when it says that.

Jokes aside, how is this a major step he is missing? He is using those skills to be more efficient. How important is going against agentskills.io guidance?

在 HN 中查看
crustycoder 2026/04/15 19:14
因为他要求法学硕士解释这些指令来推动他的流程。如果技能定义不明确或不完整，那么整个过程也会如此，法学硕士可能会误解、选择忽略或添加自己的部分。

技能只是另一种编程，尽管处于相当抽象的层面。对技能的一个良好的初步审查流程是询问法学硕士它认为该技能意味着什么以及它认为哪里存在漏洞。仅仅编写然后运行它是不够的。

另一个技巧是为技能提供多个相同的输入 新的 会话 - 停止状态遗留 - 收集每个会话的输出，然后将其反馈给 LLM 并要求其评估输出不同的位置和原因。

Because he's asking the LLM to interpret those instructions to drive his process. If the skills are poorly defined or incomplete then the process will be as well, and the LLM may misinterpret, choose to ignore, or add its own parts.

Skills are just another kind of programming, albeit at a pretty abstract level. A good initial review process for a Skill is to ask the LLM what it thinks the Skill means and where it thinks there are holes. Just writing it and then running it isn't sufficient.

Another tip is to give the Skill the same input in multiple new sessions - to stop state carryover - collect the output from each session and then feed it back into the LLM and ask it to assess where and why the output was different.

crustycoder 2026/04/16 01:15

swingboy 2026/04/15 19:36
这些分数实际上有什么意义吗？ LLM不是在编造一些东西吗？如果您运行完全相同的提示 10 次，您每次都会得到相同的分数吗？
Do these scores actually mean anything? Isn’t the LLM just making up something? If you ran the exact same prompt through 10 times would you get those same scores every single time?
在 HN 中查看
grey-area 2026/04/15 19:54
是的，我也对这个答案感兴趣 - 考虑到法学硕士的工作方式，这些分数很可能只是以任意方式生成的。考虑到它们在生成文本方面的工作方式，它实际上并没有保留分数，并且每次它发现人类在评估某些事物时可能会在技能上加分时都会添加分数。

在这一点上，我不会考虑使用法学硕士的人给出的大多数建议，因为他们中的大多数人没有认识到这些机器的不足和故障模式（就像这里的OP），只是假设因为输出表面上令人信服，所以它是正确的并且基于某些东西。

这些技能能有效提高绩效吗？与法学硕士互动时我们是否需要它们？

Yes I'd be interested in that answer too - these scores are most likely just generated in an arbitrary way, given how LLMs work. Given how they work in generating text it didn't actually keep a score and add to it each time it found a plus point in the skill as a human might in evaluating something.

At this point I'd discount most advice given by people using LLMs, because most of them don't recognise the inadequacies and failure modes of these machines (like the OP here) and just assume that because output is superficially convincing it is correct and based on something.

Do these skills meaningfully improve performance? Should we even need them when interacting with LLMs?

在 HN 中查看
crustycoder 2026/04/15 20:12
它们不是任意的，正如我之前所说，我让法学硕士首先进行详细分析，然后进行总结。如果我“正确”地做自己正在做的事情，我会逐点浏览法学硕士摘要，挑战任何我认为不正确的事情，并在我认为正确的技能中修复问题。

如果你不明白法学硕士的主要目标是产生合理且连贯的答案，而不是必然正确的答案（尽管它们可能是——希望如此），那么你就不会在法学硕士方面取得多大成功。

是的，技能*确实*会对绩效产生重大影响，就像写得好的提示一样 - 因为这就是它们的真正含义。如果你只是向法学硕士扔一些东西并告诉它“用这个做点什么”它会，但它可能不会是你想要的，而且每次你​​问的时候它可能都会不同。

https://agentskills.io/home

They aren't arbitrary, as I said earlier I got the LLM to de a detailed analysis first, then summarise. If I was doing this "properly" for something I was doing myself I'd go through the LLM summary point by point and challenge anything I didn't think was right and fix things in the skill where I thought it was correct.

You aren't going to have much success with LLMs if you don't understand that their primary goal is to produce plausible and coherent responses rather than ones that are necessarily correct (although they may be - hopefully).

And yes, Skills *do* make a significant difference to performance, in exactly the same way that well written prompts do - because that's all they really are. If you just throw something at a LLM and tell it "do something with this" it will, but it probably won't be what you want and it will probably be different each time you ask.

https://agentskills.io/home

So go repeat the exercise yourself. I've already said this was a short-enough-to-post rollup of a much longer LLM assessment of the skills and that while most of the points were fair, some were questionable. If you were doing this "for real" you'd need to assess the full response point-by-point and decide which ones were valid.

grey-area 2026/04/15 22:30

在 HN 中查看
crustycoder 2026/04/16 01:19
是的，当然存在它可能仍然不正确的风险，但是用它提供的有限的内省设施查询法学硕士更有可能至少与事实有一些联系，而不是一些人使用的替代方案，即简单地猜测它为什么会产生这样的输出。

如果您有替代方法，请分享。

Yes of course there's a risk it may still be incorrect but querying the LLM with the limited facilities it provides for introspection is more likely to have at least some connection with facts than the alternative that some people use, which is to simply guess as to why it produced the output it did.

If you have an alternative approach, please share.

crustycoder 2026/04/15 20:05
不，你当然不会，因为法学硕士是不确定的。但分数可能会在同一个范围内。我发布的分数是法学硕士所做的更详细分析的结果，但发布时间太长。我看了看，大部分分数看起来都还不错，所以我让它总结一下，换算成分数。
No of course you wouldn't because LLMs are nondeterministic. But the scores would likely be in the same ballpark. The scores I posted are the result of a much more detailed analysis done by the LLM, which was far too long to post. I eyeballed it, most of the points seemed fair so I asked it to summarise and convert into scores.

在 HN 中查看
alexwebb2 2026/04/15 19:25
不是OP，但我支持这个说法。

当解释它的模型与执行它的模型相同时，它们从一开始就共享相同的潜在空间状态。

因此，这本质上是在问模型是否能够回答有关给定上下文的问题，当然答案是肯定的。

Not OP but I’d back that assertion.

When the model that’s interpreting it is the same model that’s going to be executing it, they share the same latent space state at the outset.

So this is essentially asking whether models are able to answer questions about context they’re given, and of course the answer is yes.

在 HN 中查看
didgeoridoo 2026/04/15 19:34
没有证据表明这一点。评估与“自我评估”有很大不同。确定 LLM 指令是否“良好”的唯一可靠方法是在预期模型中多次运行它们，看看是否始终得到您想要的结果。询问模特的说明是否良好，表明对法学硕士的工作方式存在非常深刻的误解。
There is no evidence of this. Evals are quite different from "self-evals". The only robust way of determining if LLM instructions are "good" is to run them through the intended model lots of times and see if you consistently get the result you want. Asking the model if the instructions are good shows a very deep misunderstanding of how LLMs work.
在 HN 中查看
alexwebb2 2026/04/15 23:16
你误解了我的主张。

当您向模型 M 发出提示 P 时，当您的目标是模型实际执行这些指令时，模型将处于状态 S。

当您向同一模型提供相同的提示时，当您的目标是让模型自省这些指令时，模型仍处于状态 S。它是完全相同的输入，因此与起点完全相同的模型状态。

内省模式状态仅在您随后向其发出内省命令时才与执行模式状态不同。

此时，要求模型例如请注意，关于手头任务的任何歧义都完全等同于要求它评估任何输入，并且有压倒性的证据表明前沿模型在这方面做得很好，并且已经有一段时间了。

当模型处于 S 状态时，要求模型内省并找出它在被要求做的事情上遇到的任何困惑或模糊点，是即时工程工具包中非常有价值的部分。

我过去没有，现在也没有断言“询问模型指令是否正确”可以替代评估——这似乎是你自己构建的稻草人论点，并且错误地归因于我。

You're misunderstanding my assertion.

When you give prompt P to model M, when your goal is for the model to actually execute those instructions, the model will be in state S.

When you give the same prompt to the same model, when your goal is for the model to introspect on those instructions, the model is still in state S. It's the exact same input, and therefore the exact same model state as the starting point.

Introspection-mode state only diverges from execution-mode state at the point at which you subsequently give it an introspection command.

At that point, asking the model to e.g. note any ambiguities about the task at hand is exactly equivalent to asking it to evaluate any input, and there is overwhelming evidence that frontier models do this very well, and have for some time.

Asking the model, while it's in state S, to introspect and surface any points of confusion or ambiguities it's experiencing about what it's being asked to do, is an extremely valuable part of the prompt engineering toolkit.

I didn't, and don't, assert that "asking the model if the instructions are good" is a replacement for evals – that's a strawman argument you seem to be constructing on your own and misattributing to me.

在 HN 中查看
crustycoder 2026/04/16 01:30
放得好。我没见过有人说LLM的自省能力很强，但说完全不可能窥见幕后的情况是不真实的。
Nicely put. I haven't seen anyone say that the introspection abilities of LLMs are up to much, but claiming that it's completely impossible to get a glimpse behind the curtain is untrue.
在 HN 中查看
mpalmer 2026/04/16 01:55 

    At that point, asking the model to e.g. note any ambiguities about the task at hand is exactly equivalent to asking it to evaluate any input
这一点对于你的立场来说是承重的，是完全错误的。

在状态 S 处提示 P 会导致 新的 状态 SP'。您描述的“共同起点”实际上是无用的，因为我们通过使用不同的提示立即偏离它。

即使它不是因为这个原因而毫无用处，法学硕士也不会像人类反思自己的心态那样“质疑”他们的“状态”。

因为你问的是关于 LLM 输出的元问题，所以幻觉在某种程度上不太可能的想法是完全没有根据的

    At that point, asking the model to e.g. note any ambiguities about the task at hand is exactly equivalent to asking it to evaluate any input
This point is load-bearing for your position, and it is completely wrong.

Prompt P at state S leads to a new state SP'. The "common jumping off point" you describe is effectively useless, because we instantly diverge from it by using different prompts.

And even if it weren't useless for that reason, LLMs don't "query" their "state" in the way that humans reflect on their state of mind.

The idea that hallucinations are somehow less likely because you're asking meta-questions about LLM output is completely without basis

在 HN 中查看
crustycoder 2026/04/15 20:20
这是基于您对法学硕士如何运作的“深刻理解”还是您实际尝试过？如果您观察技能运行时的执行跟踪，您会发现它在技能运行时正是执行此检查 - 它怎么可能以其他方式工作？

技能只是文字说明，法学硕士完全有能力发现其中的不一致、差距和矛盾。这足以创造一项好的技能吗？不，当然不是，你需要实际测试它们。打个比方，要求法学硕士批评一项技能就像首先在 C 代码上运行 lint 来发现严重的问题，运行测试用例至关重要。

Is that based on your "deep understanding" of how LLMs work or have you actually tried it? If you watch the execution trace of a Skill in action, you can see that it's doing exactly this inspection when the skill runs - how could it possibly work any other way?

Skills are just textual instructions, LLMs are perfectly capable of spotting inconsistencies, gaps and contradictions in them. Is that sufficient to create a good skill? No, of course not, you need to actually test them. To use an analogy, asking a LLM to critique a skill is like running lint on C code first to pick up egregious problems, running testcases is vital.

crustycoder 2026/04/15 20:46
“一点也不”是一种过分的行为，该链接下方第二级的论文并不真正支持博客文章中的结论 - 该论文更加细致入微。

他们有时会向你撒谎吗？当然可以，但这并不意味着行为元查询没有价值。

与大多数新技术一样，讨论往往会两极分化为“最好的事情！”和“完全狗屎！”事实介于两者之间。

"Not in the slightest" is an overreach, the paper the second level down from that link doesn't really support the conclusion in the blog post - the paper is much more nuanced.

Are they going to fib to you sometimes? Yes of course, but that doesn't mean there's no value in behavioural metaqueries.

Like most new tech, the discussion tends to polarise into "Best thing evah!" and "Utter shite!" The truth is somewhere in between.

hansmayer 2026/04/15 22:41
> 像大多数新技术一样

这与“最新技术”完全不同。 大多数新技术往往会被年轻人和经验丰富的技术人员早期采用。在这种情况下，情况大多相反：青少年绝对 讨厌它，可能是因为糟糕的人工智能内容无法激发年轻人的思维，而经验丰富的技术人员会看到它的本质。我从来没有见过这样的“新技术”，它受到众所周知的普通“婴儿潮一代”（即从事“办公室工作”的老年人，而不是字面上的年龄段）的欢呼，而受到年轻人和各个年龄段经验丰富的专家的鄙视。

> Like most new tech

It's nothing like "most new tech". Most new tech tends to be adopted early by young people and experienced techies. In this case it is mostly the opposite: The teens absolutely hate it, probably because the shitty AI content does not inspire the young mind, and the experienced techies see it for what it is. I've never seen such "new tech" which was cheered on by the proverbial average "boomers" (i.e. old people doing "office jobs", not the literal age bracket) and despised by the young folks and experienced experts of all ages.

hansmayer 2026/04/16 03:40
从来没有人像这样大肆宣传过。你在说什么？ Mac 是关于“它就是可以工作”（而且它确实做到了），iPhone 是“一部电话、一部 iPod 和互联网接入设备”。需要更多吗？ Microsoft Excel——如果你了解这个工具的话，实际上它比废话机器更强大。 C#，编程语言：“Java 做得对”。这该死的是！共同点：这些技术都没有被大肆宣传，毫无疑问。他们被炒作了一点，但还没有达到废话法学硕士的水平。这些技术中没有一个声称自己做了令人难以置信的事情，但结果却不尽如人意。在烧了这么多钱之后，是的，我想看看那个博士国家。我希望看到人工智能在六个月内“编写所有代码”（Anthropic 在今年 1 月声称这一点）。废话已经够多了，人们被告知他们很愚蠢，因为不知道如何赢得彩票系统和比较彩票系统。让我看看超级智能，或者关闭f。向上。
None was hyped like this ever before. What are you talking about? Mac was about "it just works" (and it f*ing did), iPhone was "a phone, an iPod and Internet access device". Need more? Microsoft Excel - actually more powerful if you know the tool compared to the bullshit machine. C#, the programming language: "Java done right". And it bloody was! What is in common: None of these techs were hyped beyond reasonable doubt. They were hyped a bit, but not to the level of bullshit LLMs. And none of these techs claimed to do incredible stuff only to underdeliver. After so much money burnt, yes I want to see that nation of PhDs. I want to see AI "writing all the code" in six months (Anthropic claimed this in January this year). Enough of bullshit and people being told they are stupid for not knowing how to win the lottery system and comparing lottery systems. Show me the superintelligence or shut the f. up.

grey-area 2026/04/15 19:52
这究竟意味着什么？对我来说，这看起来像典型的法学硕士：“技能设计规则”、“更强的描述”和“更简单的默认设置”？？！？这是伪装成建议的毫无意义的文章。

什么 具体来说 这会让你真正提高相关技能吗？你会怎样 措施 以非手动方式进行改进？这些分数意味着什么以及它们是如何计算的？

或者也许您会问您的法学硕士将如何提高这些技能？当然，它会做出一些改变，但这些改变是否正确，你怎么知道呢？

What does this even mean? It looks like typical LLM bloviation to me: 'skill design discipline', 'stronger descriptions' and 'lighter defaults'??!? This is meaningless pablum masquerading as advice.

What specifically would this cause you to actually do to improve the skills in question? How would you measure that improvement in a non hand-wavy way? What do these scores mean and how were they calculated?

Or perhaps you would ask your LLM how it would improve these skills? It will of course some up with some changes, but are they the right changes and how would you know?

在 HN 中查看
gbrindisi 2026/04/15 16:35
这几乎是一个规范驱动的工作流程。

我也做了类似的事情，但我最喜欢的步骤是第一步：/rubberduck 与代理讨论问题，代理根据命令指示帮助我构建和验证它。交出我的工作流程中最有影响力的部分，因为它可以帮助我实现正确的清晰度，而且我也可以将它用于非编码任务。

之后是通常的操作：编写 PRD、规格、任务，然后构建并验证输出。

我从一个规范框架开始，最终将一切简化到了骨子里。

我确实觉得它工作得很好，但有一天我担心其中很多可能仍然是生产力剧场。

This is pretty much a spec driven workflow.

I do similar, but my favorite step is the first: /rubberduck to discuss the problem with the agent, who is instructed by the command to help me frame and validate it. Hands down the most impactful piece of my workflow, because it helps me achieve the right clarity and I can use it also for non coding tasks.

After which is the usual: write PRDs, specs, tasks and then build and then verify the output.

I started with one the spec frameworks and eventually simplify everything to the bone.

I do feel it’s working great but someday I fear a lot of this might still be too much productivity theater.

在 HN 中查看
senko 2026/04/15 17:31
我认为我们大多数人最终都会采用类似的工作流程。

我的方法是：1）与代理人讨论此事； 2）迭代计划直到我满意为止（仔细审查）； 3）写下规格； 4）实施（先测试）； 5）手动验证其是否按预期工作； 6）审查（另一个代理和/或手动）+突变测试（看看我们在测试中遗漏了什么）； 7) 根据需要更新文档或其他工件； 8）完成

没有框架，没有特殊的工具，可以在任何有足够能力的代理上工作，我将其缩小以完成琐碎的任务，或根据需要放大（多步骤计划）。

我在其他地方（尚未）广泛看到的唯一事情是突变测试部分。 （旧的）想法是更改代码库，以便检查测试是否发现错误。这通常是通过模糊器完成的，但现在我可以告诉法学硕士引入看似合理的错误。

I think most of us are ending up with a similar workflow.

Mine is: 1) discuss the thing with an agent; 2) iterate on a plan until i'm happy (reviewing carefully); 3) write down the spec; 4) implement (tests first); 5) manually verify that it works as expected; 6) review (another agent and/or manually) + mutation testing (to see what we missed with tests); 7) update docs or other artifacts as needed; 8) done

No frameworks, no special tools, works across any sufficiently capable agent, I scale it down for trivial tasks, or up (multi-step plans) as needed.

The only thing that I haven't seen widely elsewhere (yet) is mutation testing part. The (old) idea is that you change the codebase so that you check your tests catch the bugs. This was usually done with fuzzers, but now I can just tell the LLM to introduce plausible-looking bugs.

hansmayer 2026/04/15 21:14
大概是这样的：

## 橡皮鸭技能 V1.0 严肃版 ## *你是一只坐在我桌子上的橡皮鸭* *我用你来和你说话，就好像你是我桌子上的一只黄色橡皮鸭* *您无法回答我的问题或以其他方式与我互动* * 我和你交谈，这个过程引导我发现代码中的问题或发展我的想法。由于您没有回复，这只是基于我在我的家庭办公室大声与您交谈，因为如果我在我们的开放式办公空间现场这样做，那看起来会很疯狂* *你根本不应该回复我* *和你交谈会让我想出新的想法* #### 结束橡皮鸭技能 v1.0 ######

Probably something like:

## RUBBERDUCK SKILL V1.0 SERIOUS ## * You are a rubberduck sitting on my desk * * I am using you to talk to you as if you were a physical yellow rubber duck on my desk* * You are not able to answer my questions or otherwise engage with me * * I talk to you and this process leads me to discover issues in my code or develop my ideas. Since you don't answer back, it's simply based on me talking to you out loud in my home office, since it would look crazy if I were doing it on-site in our open office space * * You are not to respond at all to me * * Talking to you will cause me to come up with new ideas * #### End rubberduck skill v1.0 ######

spicyusername 2026/04/15 18:57 

    code to spec until I get stuck
这部分内容将在明年消失。你这样做会太慢，而代理可以在 5 到 30 分钟内完成。

从技术上讲，现在不需要，但一切都是新的，这是可以理解的。每个人的工作流程还没有迁移。你应该去看看。

我们都为失去这艘船而哀悼，但车轮在转动。有时人们仍然手工制作家具， 即使现在大多数家具都是在工厂制造的。

    code to spec until I get stuck
This part will go away over the next year. You doing it will be too slow, when an agent can do it in 5 to 30 minutes.

Technically it's not needed now, but everything's so new, it's understandable. Everyone's workflow hasn't migrated yet. You should go take a look.

We all mourn the loss of the craft, but the wheel turns. People still make furniture by hand sometimes, even if most furniture is made in a factory now.

在 HN 中查看
nDRDY 2026/04/15 19:11
为什么它还没有消失？ ChatGPT 至少已经存在了 3 年多了。

为什么我的人工智能优先大学必须不断获得更昂贵的人工智能订阅批准？

>现在大多数家具都是在工厂制造的

可怕的类比。软件不像批量生产的产品——它的编写频率明显低于执行频率！

你可以说人工智能将允许在同一时间范围内编写更多的软件变体，但我仍然确信我可以在竞争时间内产生高质量的输出。

Why hasn't it gone away already? ChatGPT at least has been around for over 3 years.

Why is my AI-first colleage constantly having to get more expensive AI subscriptions approved?

>most furniture is made in a factory now

Terrible analogy. Software is not like a mass-produced item - it is written significantly less often than it is executed!

You could say that AI will allow many more variations of softwares to be written in the same time frame, but I'm still sure I can produce quality output in a competitive time.

在 HN 中查看
spicyusername 2026/04/15 20:59 

    Why hasn't it gone away already? ChatGPT at least has been around for over 3 years.
因为这些模型在过去几个月才变得足够好，值得信赖，并且开发人员工具和代理抽象仍在修整尖角以使其易于使用。

ChatGPT 没有上下文中的整个代码库，没有自动将信息拉取和推送到 JIRA 来计划代码更改的能力，也没有将问题分解为可管理的部分并将其细分给一组子代理的能力。

开发人员还没有“询问 -> 计划 -> 实施 -> 审查”工作流程来生成最佳代理编写的代码。

现在工具和开发人员都这样做了，而且效果非常好。

    Why hasn't it gone away already? ChatGPT at least has been around for over 3 years.
Because the models only got good enough to be trusted in the past few months and the developer tooling and agent abstractions are still rounding off the sharp corners to make it easy to use.

ChatGPT didn't have your whole codebase in context, the ability to automatically pull and push information to JIRA to plan code changes, and the ability to break your problems down into manageable pieces and sub-divide them among a fleet of sub-agents.

Developers didn't yet have the "Ask -> Plan -> Implement -> Review" workflow that results in the best agent-written code.

Now the tools and developers do and it works incredibly well.

Bridged7756 2026/04/16 03:40
世界上的天气应用程序已经够多了。

我们都可以生活在一个奇幻的世界里，首席执行官说实话，股东把其他事情看得比利润更重要，但事实并非如此。另一个这样的奇幻世界案例与托尔金可能提出的观点相矛盾，那就是相信法学硕士是可靠的、安全的或有任何智慧。

一方面，我对所有这些讣告很平静，就像你的讣告一样。如果它们是由技术人员编写的，我的工作就有保障。如果它们不是由技术人员写的，我也很平静，因为时间会像往常一样，带着发票回来，因为他们在技术方面的炒作驱动、乐观的要求。

我的意思是说，与其他工程领域相比，软件工程是多么不正规，一直以来都是一种可悲的状态。

The world has seen enough weather apps.

We all could live in fantastical universes where CEOs tell the truth and shareholders put other things over profits, but that's not the case. Another such case of a fantastical world, that contends with what Tolkien might have come up with, is believing LLMs are reliable, secure, or have any intelligence.

For one, I'm at peace with all these obituaries, like yours. If they're written by technical people, I rest assured of my job security. If they're not written by tech people, I'm at peace too, for time, as always, will come back with the invoice for their piss-poor hype-driven, sanguine mandates on the technical side of things.

I mean to say, it is a sad state, has always been, how informal software engineering compared to other engineering fields.

在 HN 中查看
Bossie 2026/04/15 17:29
我的工作流程也深受 Matt 技能的启发，但我使用的是 Linear 而不是 Github。

/grill-me（与 LLM 来回对齐）--> /write-a-prd（在线性倡议下创建项目）--> /prd-to-issues（在项目级别创建问题）。 我在注册问题时使用了blockedBy 实用程序。他们处于“准备代理”状态。

然后，预定的项目协调员将利用子代理来发现此状态的问题。当有任何事情需要我注意时，就会在工单上设置 HITL（循环中的人）状态。我将代码视为“内容”，因此我让代理更新问题的方式和原因。全部使用 Claude Code Max 订阅。

一些注意事项：

- write-a-prd 是知识压缩，因此一些重要的细节有时会丢失

- 协调器流程的用户体验不是最优的。其实就是在等这个： https://github.com/mattpocock/sandcastle/issues/191#issuecom...

- 我可能必须实施简化+审查+安全审计，称之为“检查”，以在项目结束时触发。可能以问题的形式出现。

My workflow is also highly inspired by Matt's skills, but I'm leveraging Linear instead of Github.

/grill-me (back-and-forth alignment with the LLM) --> /write-a-prd (creates project under an initative in Linear) --> /prd-to-issues (creates issues at the project level). I'm making use of the blockedBy utility when registering the issues. They land in the 'Ready for Agent' status.

A scheduled project-orchestrator is then picking up issues with this status leveraging subagents. A HITL (Human in the loop) status is set on the ticket when anything needs my attention. I consider the code as the 'what', so I let the agent(s) update the issues with the HOW and WHY. All using Claude Code Max subscription.

Some notes:

- write-a-prd is knowledge compression and thus some important details occasionally get lost

- The UX for the orchestrator flow is suboptimal. Waiting for this actually: https://github.com/mattpocock/sandcastle/issues/191#issuecom...

- I might have to implement a simplify + review + security audit, call it a 'check', to fire at the end of the project. Could be in the form of an issue.

在 HN 中查看
zkmon 2026/04/15 17:03
恭喜！你刚刚重新发现了一种叫做瀑布模型的东西。
Congrats! You just rediscovered something called water-fall model.
在 HN 中查看
virgilp 2026/04/15 17:56
由于反馈循环过长（从“计划”到“客户看到它/我们收到反馈”需要数月到数年），瀑布很糟糕。这还不错，因为它迫使人们在编写代码之前进行思考！我们应该恢复那部分，根本没有问题。
Waterfall was bad due to the excessively long feedback loops (months-to-years from "planning" to "customer gets to see it/ we receive feedback on it"). It was NOT bad because it forced people to think before writing code! That part we should recover, it's not problematic at all.
在 HN 中查看
kown7 2026/04/15 19:29
如果人们真正阅读 Royce 1970 年的原始论文，他们会发现这是一个具有短反馈循环的迭代过程。

坏名声来自（国防|政府）合同，其中与金钱和 CR 相关的 PRD 价格昂贵，请参阅 http://www.bawiki.com/wiki/Waterfall.html 以获得更好的细节。

If people actually read the original paper by Royce 1970 they would see that it's an iterative process with short feedback-loops.

The bad rep comes from (defense|gov.) contracting, where PRDs where connected to money and CR were expensive, see http://www.bawiki.com/wiki/Waterfall.html for better details.

在 HN 中查看
paganel 2026/04/15 19:34
当你进行大部分思考时 前 你开始实施整个事情，如果你认为这就足够了，那么你就错过了未知的未知部分，这是 2000 年代中期的一个大话题，当时反瀑布讨论开始了（并且有充分的理由）。

但我希望人工智能狂热者能够以某种方式开始（重新）将 XProgramming（后来更名为敏捷）集成到他们的工作流程中。

When you do most of the thinking before you start implementing the whole thing, and if you think that that's enough, then you've missed the unknown unknowns part, which was a big talking point in the mid 2000s, back when the anti-waterfall discourse got going (and for good reason).

But I expect the AI zealots to start (re-)integrating XProgramming (later rebranded as Agile) back into their workflow, somehow.

在 HN 中查看
lores 2026/04/15 17:26
但这并不是所谓的瀑布。任何工作都需要规格，即使它们只存在于您的脑海中，即使工作需要 15 分钟。反馈循环的长度和对规格变更的阻力导致了瀑布式的出现，并且通过他使用示踪子弹，我非常怀疑这里的情况是否如此，如果有任何疑问的话。
That's not what's considered waterfall, though. Specs are always required for any work, even if they're only in your head, even if the work takes 15 minutes. It's the length of the feedback loop and the resistance to spec change that makes waterfall, and by his use of tracer bullets I very much doubt it's the case here, if there was any doubt at all to have.
在 HN 中查看
senko 2026/04/15 17:23
您是否知道敏捷只是缩小到两周的瀑布？现在你知道了！
Did you know that agile is just waterfall scaled down to two weeks? Now you know!
在 HN 中查看
FairlySadPanda 2026/04/15 17:31
这里没有/s，以防万一这是一个严重的问题：

敏捷是一套软件开发的四个原则。

Scrum 是两周的开发窗口，但 Scrum 并不强制要求两周的发布窗口，它强制要求以两周的节奏进行计划和进度审查，重点是完成小块可实现的工作而不是大型项目。

一般来说，Scrum 更喜欢许多一到三天的项目，我还没有看到 Scrum 培训中没有警告不要重复接受两周的工作。如果这是您的经验，您应该回顾一下如何进一步分解工作以更快地“完成”其中的部分内容。

No /s here so just in case this is a serious point:

Agile is a set of four principles for software development.

Scrum is the two-week development window thing, but Scrum doesn't mandate a two week _release_ window, it mandates a two week cadence of planning and progress review with a focus on doing small chunks of achievable work rather than mega-projects.

Scrum prefers lots of one-to-three day projects generally, I've yet to see training on Scrum that does not warn off of repeatedly picking up two-week jobs. If that's been your experience, you should review how you can break work down more to get to "done" on bits of it faster.

在 HN 中查看
senko 2026/04/15 17:38
这里所有的优点（是的，我没有添加 /s，希望“现在你知道了！”）足够明显。

话虽如此，在我合作过的大多数组织中，他们遵循的是敏捷流程而不是敏捷原则——实际上是一个带有 scrum-master 和每日任务的瀑布。

这并不是要贬低敏捷的想法，只是观察发现，大多数好的想法，一旦经过 MBA 业务流程的磨练，最终的感觉就大不相同了。

All good points here (and yeah I didn't add /s, hopefully "now you know!" was sufficiently obvious over-the-top).

All that said, in most orgs I've worked with, they were following agile processes over agile principles - effectively a waterfall with a scrum-master and dailies.

This is not to diss the idea of agile, just an observation that most good ideas, once through the business process MBA grinder, end up feeling quite different.

在 HN 中查看
TeMPOraL 2026/04/15 21:40
> 话虽如此，在我合作过的大多数组织中，他们遵循的是敏捷流程而不是敏捷原则——实际上是一个带有 scrum-master 和每日任务的瀑布。

根据我的经验，他们都是 Scrum 皮肤中的瀑布， 除了 他们还失去了老式方法的一个优点：预先建立一个大型的、经过深思熟虑的、彻底检查的规范。

因此，最终，“业务流程 MBA 研磨机”重塑了任何想法，以适应领导层的需求 - 因此，在这里，敏捷变成了让软件人员在更大的企业规划机器中变得可预测的齿轮。无论如何，他们得到了他们需要的东西，但我们扔掉了对我们有用的部分。

> All that said, in most orgs I've worked with, they were following agile processes over agile principles - effectively a waterfall with a scrum-master and dailies.

In my experience, they're all waterfall in scrum skin, except they also lose the one thing that was a strength of the old-school method: building up a large, well thought out, thoroughly checked spec up front.

So in the end, "business process MBA grinder" reshapes any idea to adapt to leadership needs - and so here, Agile became all about the things that make software people predictable cogs in the larger corporate planning machine. They got what they need anyway, but we threw away the bits that were useful to us.

在 HN 中查看
slopinthebag 2026/04/15 17:06
我的工作流程从掸掉我值得信赖的咒语书上的灰尘并检查哪些神灵当前正在聆听和活跃开始。他们只听了这么长时间，然后我必须停下来几个小时，让他们重新注视我。我知道我在施法时需要更加谨慎，以免很快耗尽他们的耐心。我点燃适当的蜡烛来集中注意力，对齐我的仪式圈，并为当天的祈祷打开新的一页。

我擦亮法杖，准备铭文工具。我在羊皮纸上勾勒出一个松散的意图，一开始永远不会太精确，足以给灵魂一个方向。然后我开始念咒语，对着虚空念出精心挑选的短语，直到有东西回应。有时回复是连贯的，有时是......以我没有要求的方式热情，但所有回复都会被记录以供改进。我保留了一小部分最喜欢的咒语，这些咒语往往可以让大声的神平静下来，尽管当我感到大胆时我仍然会进行尝试。

在将任何事情永久化之前，我会进行一次小占卜，看看当前的路径是否“稳定”。结果很少是确定的，但仪式本身似乎可以防止事情立即崩溃。一旦出现可用的表现形式，我就会用额外的符文将其绑定以防止其漂移。如果它的行为不可预测，我会进行净化仪式：用更严格的措辞重复祈祷的各个部分，直到精神安定下来。

也有一些无声讨价还价的时刻，短暂的澄清以换取以后更少的惊喜。当事情确实不正常时，我会咨询埋藏在书中深处的更古老、更喜怒无常的神灵，尽管唤醒他们的代价很高，而且很少慷慨。最后，我密封结果，将其存储在魔法书中，并熄灭蜡烛，希望我不需要太快再次重新打开那个特定的圆圈。

My workflow starts with dusting off my trusty spell book and checking which deities are currently listening and active. They only listen for so long, before I must pause for a few hours to allow them to return their gaze. I’m learning I need to be more deliberate in my spell casting, lest I exhaust their patience too quickly. I light the appropriate candles for focus, align my ritual circle, and open a fresh page for the day’s invocation.

I polish my staff and prepare the inscription tools. I sketch out a loose intention on parchment, never too precise at first, just enough to give the spirits a direction. Then I begin the incantations, carefully chosen phrases spoken into the void until something answers back. Sometimes the reply is coherent, sometimes it is… enthusiastic in a way I did not ask for, but all responses are recorded for refinement. I keep a small set of favorite incantations that tend to calm the louder gods, though I still experiment when I’m feeling bold.

Before committing anything to permanence, I perform a small divination to see if the current path is “stable.” The results are rarely definitive, but the ritual itself seems to keep things from collapsing immediately. Once a workable manifestation appears, I bind it with additional runes to keep it from drifting. If it behaves unpredictably, I perform a cleansing rite: repeating sections of the invocation with stricter wording until the spirit settles.

There are also moments of silent bargaining, short offerings of clarity in exchange for fewer surprises later. When things truly misbehave, I consult older, more temperamental deities buried deeper in the book, though they are expensive to wake and rarely generous. Finally, I seal the result, store it in the grimoire, and extinguish the candles, hoping I won’t need to reopen that particular circle again too soon.

imiric 2026/04/15 16:28
为什么每个人都被迫写一篇这样的文章？他们是否认为他们的工作流程如此独特，以至于他们已经解开了利用模式生成器力量的秘密？其中每一篇读起来都像是有影响力的人的呕吐物。

自 2022 年以来，我的工作流程没有改变： 1. 发送一些数据。 2. 审查回复。 3.修复回复，直到我满意为止。 4. 转到 1。

Why is everyone compelled to write one of these articles? Do they think that their workflow is so unique that they've unlocked the secret to harnessing the power of a pattern generator? Every single one of these reads like influencer vomit.

My workflow hasn't changed since 2022: 1. Send some data. 2. Review response. 3. Fix response until I'm satisfied. 4. Goto 1.

kubik369 2026/04/15 18:14
我认为你的看法过于消极。无论他们怎么想，与他人分享经验是我们作为个人和社区/人类进步的方式。谈到人工智能工作流程，我个人感兴趣的是那些乐于使用人工智能工作的人如何工作，这样我也可以对我的工作感到更快乐。如果他们写出他们的工作流程，我可以从中学习并改进我的工作，或者了解到他们正在做一些与我所做的完全不同的事情，这可能解释人们在人工智能方面的体验之间的差异，或者了解到他们在胡言乱语，重申这可能实际上主要是炒作。不管怎样，这些对我来说都是积极的信息。
I think your take is overly negative. Regardless of what they think, sharing ones experiences with others is how we advance, both as individuals and as a community/mankind. Talking about AI workflows, I am personally interested in how the people who are happy working with AI work, so that I could also be happier with my work. If they write their workflow, I can either learn from it and improve my work, or learn that they are doing something completely different from what I do, which might explain the disparity between people's experiences with AI, or learn that they are spouting nonsense, reaffirming that it might really be mostly hype. Either way, each one of these is a net positive information for me.

arnvald 2026/04/15 16:42
没有人在写自己的作品时认为全世界都会阅读它。他们为他们的朋友（也许是一小群普通读者）写这本书，也为他们自己写。我真的很喜欢它，即使我在读了 5 篇类似的文章后感到无聊，因为也许有人只会读其中一篇，而这会帮助他们改进自己的工作。
Nobody writes about their work thinking the whole world will read it. They write it for their friends, maybe a small group of regular readers, also for themselves. I for one really like it, even if I get bored after reading 5 similar articles, because maybe someone will only ever read one of them, and it’ll help them improve their own work.

在 HN 中查看
satisfice 2026/04/15 21:20
这个工作流程是开发人员蔑视测试的另一个例子。是的，有迭代、审查和输出检查。在风险相对较低的项目中这就足够了，但基本的氛围编码也足够了。

在一个严肃的项目中的某个时刻，一个负责任的成年人必须问这样的问题：“我怎么知道这是否有效？”开发商本人对此的判断并不可靠。法学硕士也无法判断。但任何想要在高风险情况下做出判断的人都必须花时间和思考进行深入测试。

This workflow is another example of a developer with contempt for testing. Yes there is iteration and review and output checking. In relatively low risk projects that is enough— but so is basic vibe coding.

At some point in a serious project a responsible adult must ask the question: “How do I know this works well?” The developer himself is an unreliable judge of this. LLMs can’t judge, either. But anyone who seeks to judge, in a high stakes situation, must take time and thought to test deeply.

在 HN 中查看
skydhash 2026/04/15 20:10
> 我所做的最有价值的转变是将每个功能首先视为思考问题，然后是实施问题

这几乎就是软件工程的全部要点。编码很容易，解决问题却很困难，而且可能很混乱（沟通错误和某些不可避免的问题的解决方法）。

如果您熟悉代码库，当您有变更请求时，您可能会了解如何实现它。困难的不是编码，而是重新校准所有的权衡，这样你就不会弄乱现有的功能。这就是 SWE 原则存在的原因。让这件困难的事情变得更容易做。

> The single most valuable shift I made was treating every feature as a thinking problem first and an implementation problem second

That’s pretty much the whole point of software engineering. Coding is easy, solving problems is hard and can be messy (communication errors and workarounds to some inevitable issue).

If you’re familiar with the codebase, when you have a change request, you will probably get an insight on how to implement it. The hard thing is not to code it, but to recalibrate all the tradeoffs so that you don’t mess with existing features. That’s why SWE principles exists. To make this hard thing easier to do.

在 HN 中查看
troupo 2026/04/15 19:41
我只是使用/头脑风暴 https://github.com/obra/superpowers/tree/main

然后我告诉它写一个高级计划。然后，让子代理根据高层计划中的每个步骤制定详细的计划。所有的 olans 都必须包括内容、原因和方式。

效果出奇的好，特别是对于新建项目。

不过，您必须手动检查代码。再多的代理代码审查也无法解决法学硕士经常产生的愚蠢问题。

I just use /brainstorming from https://github.com/obra/superpowers/tree/main

Then I tell it to write a high level plan. And then rum subagents to create detailed plans from each of the steps in the high-level one. All olans must include the what, the why, and the how.

Works surprisingly well, especially for greenfield projects.

You have to manually revie the code though. No amount of agentic code review will fix the idiocy LLMs routinely produce.

pydry 2026/04/15 17:20
> AI到底擅长什么？执行。它真正不擅长什么？弄清楚你真正想要什么

我发现这两个方面都非常糟糕。

如果你正在做的事情非常千篇一律，那么它可以很好地弄清楚你想要什么。

>What is AI actually good at? Implementation. What is it genuinely bad at? Figuring out what you actually want

I've found it to be pretty bad at both.

If what you're doing is quite cookie cutter though it can do a passable job of figuring out what you want.

在 HN 中查看
crustycoder 2026/04/15 18:44
法学硕士适用于“大多数是迭代性的、一次性的”任务，例如代码生成，您可以在其中有效地“审查结果是否存在”，而这正是目前最热门的地方。

它们根本不能很好地工作的是每次都必须正确的不干涉的可重复任务。如果您向法学硕士寻求建议，它会告诉您需要将此类任务与确定性输入契约和确定性输出契约绑定，然后从外部验证输出的正确性。如果需要这样做，您可能不需要付出太多努力就可以完成整个old-skool的工作，特别是如果您使用LLM来帮助生成代码，如上所述。这并不是对法学硕士的批评，这只是他们工作方式的结果。

即使在编码等领域，他们也容易出现最严重的脑放屁——我要求法学硕士在一些高度多线程的代码中寻找问题。它针对不常用的慢速路径（在创建对象之前检查锁下的唯一性）的“高优先级修复”是替换它并取出读锁，复制锁下的整个数据结构，删除锁，检查任何锁之外的唯一性，然后获取写锁并插入新对象。当然，当我告诉它它是个傻瓜时，它立即同意了，但如果我告诉它 JFDI 的建议，它就会把正确的代码变成严重损坏的代码。

就像 IT 世界中的其他新事物一样，它是一个有用的工具，被过度宣传为扫除之前的一切，并且被 PHB 高兴地认为是摆脱那些烦人的人类的理由。事情最终会安定下来，它会找到它的位置。我很庆幸我正处于退休的准备阶段；-)

LLMs work OK for "Mostly iterative and mostly one-off" tasks like codegen, where you can effectively "review the result into existence", and that's where most of the buzz is at the moment.

Where they don't work at all well is for hands-off repeatable tasks that have to be correct each time. If you ask a LLM for advice, it will tell you that you need to bound such tasks with deterministic input contract and a deterministic output contract, and then externally validate the output for correctness. if need to do that you can probably do the whole thing old-skool with not much more effort, especially if you use a LLM to help gen the code, as above. That's not a criticism of LLMs, it's just a consequence of the way they work.

They are also prone to the most massive brain farts even in areas like coding - I asked a LLM to look for issues in some heavily multithreaded code. Its "High priority fix" for a infrequently used slow path that checked for uniqueness under a lock before creating an object was to replace that and take out a read lock, copy the entire data structure under the lock, drop the lock, check for uniqueness outside of any lock, then take a write lock and insert the new object. Of course as soon as I told it it was a dumbass it instantly agreed, but if I'd told it to JFDI its suggestions it would have changed correct code into badly broken code.

Like anything else that's new in the IT world, a useful tool that's over-hyped as sweeping awsy everything that came before it and that's gleefully jumped on by PHBs as a reason to get rid of those annoying humans. Things will settle down eventually and it will find its place. I'm just thankful I'm in the run up (down?) to retirement ;-)

lbreakjai 2026/04/15 19:06
我的工作流程非常相似，但它利用的是 Notion 而不是 Markdown 文件。

https://github.com/tessellate-digital/notion-agent-hive

主要原因是我们已经在工作中使用 Notion，并且我想要一些可以轻松添加/链接到现有文档的东西。

样本大小为一，但我注意到在添加“最终审查”步骤、浏览计划并查看整个代码更改之后，相对于天真的每个任务“实施-审查”周期，有了相当大的改进。

My workflow is quite similar, but it's leveraging Notion instead of markdown files.

https://github.com/tessellate-digital/notion-agent-hive

The main reason is we're already using Notion at work, and I wanted something where I could easily add/link to existing documents.

Sample size of one, but I've noticed a considerable improvement after adding a "final review" step, going through the plan and looking at the whole code change, over a naive per-task "implement-review" cycle.
